texto: When one devotes oneself to meditation mental burdens unnecessary worries and wandering thoughts drop off one by one life seems to run smoothly and pleasantly. A student may now depend on intuition to make decisions. As one acts on intuition second thought with its dualism doubt and hesitation does not arise.
explicacao: Citação de Nyogen Senzaki sobre sentido da existência e experiência vivida. “When one devotes oneself to meditation mental burdens unnecessary worries and wandering thoughts …” articula saude-mental, doubts, uncertainties em registro de saude mental, com relevância para contextos como termino e uncertainties.

frase_original: When one devotes oneself to meditation mental burdens unnecessary worries and wandering thoughts drop off one by one life seems to run smoothly and pleasantly. A student may now depend on intuition to make decisions. As one acts on intuition second thought with its dualism doubt and hesitation does not arise.

texto: 1. A Cup of Tea Nan-in, a Japanese master during the Meiji era (1868-1912), recieved a university professor who came to inqure about Zen. Nan-in served tea. He poured his visitor's cup full, and then kept on pouring. The professor watched the overflow until he could no longer restrain himself. "It is overfull. No more will go in!" "Like this cup," Nan-in said, "you are full of your own opinions and speculations. How can I show you Zen unless you first empty your up?
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Nyogen Senzaki: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “1. A Cup of Tea Nan-in, a Japanese master during the Meiji era (1868-1912), recieved a university…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, open-mindedness, zen pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de dia-chuvoso e zen.

"Like this cup," Nan-in said, "you are full of your own opinions and speculations. How can I show you Zen unless you first empty your up?

texto: 5. If You Love, Love Openly Twenty monks and one nun, who was named Eshun, were practicing meditation with a certain Zen master. Eshun was very pretty even though her head was shaved and her dress plain. Several monks secretly fell in love with her. One of them wrote her a love letter, insisting upon a private meeting. Eshun did not reply. The following day the master gave a lecture to the group, and when it was over, Eshun arose. Addressing the one who had written her, she said: "If you really love me so much, come and embrace me now.
explicacao: Citação de Nyogen Senzaki sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “5. If You Love, Love Openly Twenty monks and one nun, who was named Eshun, were practicing medita…” articula amor, love, zen em registro de amor, com relevância para contextos como zen e zen-flesh-zen-bones.
